audit-a01.text: What does Claude's tool use API let it do?
options: Call functions you define, read real results, and reason over live system state
Only generate text from training data
audit-a01.explanation: Tool use turns Claude from a text generator into an auditor that acts on live systems.

audit-a01.text: A tool definition has which three components?
audit-a01.explanation: Name identifies it, description guides selection, input_schema defines arguments.

audit-a01.text: What does Claude use to decide when to call a specific tool?
audit-a01.explanation: Write descriptions like API-reference entries — they drive reliable selection.

audit-a01.text: In the agentic loop, who executes the tool call?
options: Your code runs it and returns a tool_result; Claude doesn't execute it
Claude executes it inside the API call
audit-a01.explanation: Claude requests a tool_use; your code executes and returns a tool_result.

audit-a01.text: Should you validate tool inputs server-side?
options: Yes — Claude can pass unexpected argument shapes; always validate
No — Claude's arguments are always perfect
audit-a01.explanation: Claude is a reasoning engine, not a trust boundary — validate every argument.

audit-a01.text: Which Capital One vector would an agentic audit polling WAF rules have caught early?
options: A deviation from the known-good WAF configuration baseline
audit-a01.explanation: Continuous baseline-deviation checks would have flagged the misconfig in hours.

audit-a01.text: How should agent permissions be scoped?
options: Only the tools and access needed for that audit's scope
audit-a01.explanation: Least privilege applies to agents exactly as to human users.

audit-a01.text: With tool-using agents, audit coverage scales with…
audit-a01.explanation: Define the logic once; the agent runs it against any number of resources.

audit-a02.text: Why must an agent enumerate endpoints before auditing an API?
options: You can't audit what you can't see — undocumented endpoints are the risk
audit-a02.explanation: Claude's first job is building a complete map of the attack surface.

audit-a02.text: What's the primary technique for finding shadow (undocumented) APIs?
options: Diff the live gateway route table against the official OpenAPI spec
audit-a02.explanation: Comparing live routes to the documented spec surfaces undocumented endpoints.

audit-a02.text: Roughly how many orgs have undocumented endpoints outside their inventory?
audit-a02.explanation: Shadow APIs are the norm, not the exception — enumeration is essential.

audit-a02.text: The Peloton exposure (one API version authed, another didn't) illustrates…
options: Auth regression — a newer version weakened authentication
audit-a02.explanation: Version drift can silently drop authentication — enumerate every version.

audit-a02.text: Where should rate limits and scope rules be enforced for an enumeration agent?
options: In the tool implementations, not in Claude's prompt reasoning
audit-a02.explanation: Guardrails belong in code; never rely on the model to self-limit.

audit-a02.text: When `stop_reason` is `end_turn` during enumeration, it means…
options: Claude has synthesized its final report and needs no more tool calls
audit-a02.explanation: end_turn signals the agent is done calling tools.

audit-a02.text: An undocumented endpoint is dangerous because…
options: It's reachable and often unmonitored, yet absent from security review
audit-a02.explanation: Unknown endpoints are unaudited attack surface.

audit-a02.text: A continuous endpoint-discovery agent helps by…
options: Re-mapping the API on every release so new shadow endpoints are caught fast
audit-a02.explanation: Continuous diffing catches drift as it's introduced.

audit-a03.text: What's the main limitation of regex-only secret scanners like truffleHog?
options: They catch well-formatted secrets but miss obfuscated or context-dependent ones
audit-a03.explanation: Regex catches patterns; Claude adds the contextual understanding regex lacks.

audit-a03.text: How does Claude improve on pattern-only secret detection?
options: It reasons about context — recognizing a secret even when it doesn't match a known pattern
audit-a03.explanation: Context understanding catches obfuscated/unusual secrets regex misses.

audit-a03.text: Why must secret scanning cover git history, not just the current tree?
options: A secret committed earlier remains in history on every clone and fork
audit-a03.explanation: A committed secret lives in history until rotated and history is rewritten.

audit-a03.text: Which signal helps detect a secret that doesn't match a known format?
options: High entropy combined with surrounding context (variable names, usage)
audit-a03.explanation: Entropy plus context flags credentials regex rules don't recognize.

options: Repos, CI/CD variables, container images, file shares, and scripts
audit-a03.explanation: Secrets hide across the whole supply chain.

audit-a03.text: When a live secret is found, the first action is…
audit-a03.explanation: Exposure means rotation; deletion alone leaves it in history.

options: Fast regex/entropy gates plus Claude's contextual reasoning for the hard cases
audit-a03.explanation: Layer cheap pattern checks with semantic reasoning.

audit-a03.text: Why is an undetected committed secret so dangerous?
options: It's a working credential anyone with repo access (now or later) can use
audit-a03.explanation: A leaked credential is a live key into your systems.

audit-a04.text: Why build a complete cloud inventory before auditing?
options: You can't audit resources you don't know exist
audit-a04.explanation: Discovery across accounts and regions is the foundation of cloud audit.

audit-a04.text: A thorough cloud discovery must cover…
options: All accounts and all regions, not just the primary one
audit-a04.explanation: Resources hide in unused regions and forgotten accounts.

audit-a04.text: Why does cloud inventory need continuous re-discovery?
options: Configurations drift — new resources appear and settings change constantly
audit-a04.explanation: The cloud is a configuration, and configurations drift.

audit-a04.text: Why are untracked 'shadow' cloud resources risky?
options: They're unmonitored and often misconfigured, yet still internet-reachable

audit-a04.text: How does an agent enumerate cloud resources?
options: Via provider API calls (list/describe) across accounts and regions
audit-a04.explanation: Cloud provider APIs expose the full resource inventory programmatically.

options: Comparing it to a known-good baseline to spot deviations
audit-a04.explanation: Deviation from baseline is what a continuous audit flags.

audit-a04.text: The stage's framing 'the cloud is not a place, it's a configuration' means…
options: Security depends on the config state, which must be continuously verified
audit-a04.explanation: Misconfiguration is the dominant cloud risk; verify config continuously.

audit-a04.text: The value of an agentic cloud cartographer is…
options: A continuously refreshed inventory that catches new/changed resources fast
audit-a04.explanation: Continuous discovery keeps the audit current as the cloud changes.

audit-a05.text: What does an IAM policy analysis agent do?
options: Reads access-control policies and flags over-permissive grants
audit-a05.explanation: IAM policies are the access-control layer; Claude reads them fast and at scale.

audit-a05.text: Which policy statement is a top finding?
options: Action:* on Resource:* — full access to everything
audit-a05.explanation: Wildcard action and resource grants are maximal over-permissioning.

audit-a05.text: Why analyze combinations of permissions, not just individual ones?
options: Certain combinations form privilege-escalation paths (e.g. iam:PassRole + ec2:RunInstances)
audit-a05.explanation: Escalation paths emerge from permission interactions.

audit-a05.text: What's the standard an IAM audit measures against?
options: Least privilege — only the permissions each principal actually needs
audit-a05.explanation: Least privilege is the benchmark; excess grants are findings.

audit-a05.text: Why use an agent for IAM review?
options: It reads hundreds of policies faster and more consistently than any human
audit-a05.explanation: Policy analysis at scale is exactly where agents beat manual review.

audit-a05.text: Which IAM hygiene issue should the agent flag?
options: Access keys older than 90 days on active users
audit-a05.explanation: Long-lived keys are a standard finding to rotate.

audit-a05.text: For applications, what does IAM best practice favor?
options: Roles with temporary credentials over users with long-lived keys
audit-a05.explanation: Roles issue short-lived credentials with nothing to leak.

options: A single compromised principal then has far more reach than its job requires
audit-a05.explanation: Excess privilege turns one compromise into a broad breach.

options: A standard protocol for connecting AI models to tools and data — 'USB for AI tools'
audit-a06.explanation: MCP standardizes tool/resource access: one protocol, many systems.

audit-a06.text: What does a standard protocol like MCP give you?
options: Reusable tool servers that work across any MCP-compatible client
audit-a06.explanation: One protocol means tools built once work everywhere.

options: Tools and resources a model can invoke through the protocol
audit-a06.explanation: MCP servers publish tools/resources for the client model to use.

audit-a06.text: Why scan MCP tool results before the model acts on them?
options: Retrieved content can carry prompt injection that hijacks the agent
audit-a06.explanation: Tool results are untrusted data — scan for injection.

audit-a06.text: How should MCP tool permissions be scoped?
options: To the minimum each tool needs — same least-privilege rule as any integration
audit-a06.explanation: Scope MCP tools tightly; an over-permissioned tool is an attack surface.

audit-a06.text: How does MCP help agentic auditing?
options: It lets one audit agent plug into many systems through a common interface
It removes the need for tools
audit-a06.explanation: Standardized connectivity lets agents audit diverse systems uniformly.

audit-a06.text: An MCP tool's output should be treated as…
options: Untrusted input that must be validated/scanned
audit-a06.explanation: Validate MCP output like any external input.

audit-a06.text: The point of 'one protocol, every system' is…
options: Interoperability — build the tool once, use it across clients and models
audit-a06.explanation: Standardization maximizes reuse across the ecosystem.

audit-a07.text: Why is reviewing a `terraform plan` so valuable?
options: You catch misconfigurations in the code before any infrastructure is deployed
audit-a07.explanation: Shift-left: a finding in a PR costs an hour; in production it can cost millions.

audit-a07.text: How does agentic IaC review fit the CI/CD pipeline?
options: A required gate: plan → JSON → Claude review → block merge on critical findings
audit-a07.explanation: It sits between the code change and deployment as a required check.

audit-a07.text: What does Claude add over rule-based scanners like tfsec/Checkov?
options: Semantic reasoning about intent — multi-resource risk and org-specific policy
audit-a07.explanation: It reasons about effect, not just a fixed rule library.

audit-a07.text: Why is the planned diff richer than scanning static config?
options: It distinguishes a fix (encrypted false→true) from a regression (public false→true)
audit-a07.explanation: Reading the change in context separates improvements from regressions.

audit-a07.text: What multi-resource pattern should IaC review catch?
options: A public S3 bucket plus an over-permissive IAM role forming a data-exfil path
audit-a07.explanation: Interacting resources create risk neither flags alone.

audit-a07.text: Beyond config, what should IaC review flag in the files themselves?
options: Hardcoded internal CIDRs, VPC IDs, and endpoints that help an attacker map you
audit-a07.explanation: IaC files can leak reconnaissance-grade detail if exfiltrated.

audit-a07.text: What makes IaC review feedback most valuable long-term?
options: It returns the exact Terraform block to paste in, training the team over time
audit-a07.explanation: Specific, actionable fixes reduce findings in future PRs.

audit-a07.text: How should Claude and the rules-based scanners be combined?
options: Run tfsec/Checkov first as a cheap gate, then Claude for the subtle findings
audit-a07.explanation: Layer fast rules with semantic reasoning to balance cost and depth.

options: Evidence gathering is ~60% of audit time; agents do it in minutes
audit-a08.explanation: Automating the biggest time cost is where agents pay off most.

audit-a08.text: How does an agent collect compliance evidence?
options: Automated API calls to cloud, IdP, and SaaS systems on a schedule
It's timestamped and tamper-evident, with a record of how it was gathered
audit-a08.explanation: Evidence must be verifiable and immutable to be relied on.

audit-a08.text: An advantage of agentic evidence collection is…
options: It runs identically every cycle, eliminating human inconsistency
audit-a08.explanation: Repeatable collection makes audits consistent and re-runnable.

options: Specific framework controls (SOC 2, ISO 27001, etc.) it satisfies
audit-a08.explanation: Each piece of evidence should map to the control it proves.

audit-a08.text: Why collect evidence continuously rather than at audit time?
options: It catches control drift between audits and slashes prep time
audit-a08.explanation: Continuous collection finds drift early and reduces crunch.

audit-a08.text: Automated evidence collection lets you cover…
options: Far more controls and systems than manual gathering allows
audit-a08.explanation: Automation expands coverage without more headcount.

audit-a08.text: The core value of an evidence-collection agent is…
options: Turning the slowest, most manual part of audit into fast, repeatable automation
audit-a08.explanation: It automates the 60% that used to be manual toil.

audit-a09.text: What does an orchestration pipeline of agents enable?
options: One agent audits a control; a pipeline of agents audits an entire organization
audit-a09.explanation: Composing agents scales from a single control to org-wide coverage.

audit-a09.text: How is a multi-agent audit typically organized?
options: Specialized sub-agents per domain (IAM, network, data) coordinated by an orchestrator
audit-a09.explanation: Domain sub-agents under an orchestrator scale and stay focused.

audit-a09.text: What does the orchestrator do with sub-agent results?
options: Aggregates and deduplicates findings into a unified report
audit-a09.explanation: The orchestrator consolidates findings across domains.

options: Only the permissions for its specific domain
audit-a09.explanation: Scoping per sub-agent contains the blast radius.

audit-a09.text: A benefit of a multi-agent pipeline is…
options: Domains can be audited in parallel, covering the org quickly
audit-a09.explanation: Parallel sub-agents make org-wide audits fast.

audit-a09.text: Why is an orchestrated pipeline valuable for recurring audits?
options: It re-runs the same coverage every cycle with no extra labor
audit-a09.explanation: Define once, run every cycle — the agentic audit advantage.

audit-a09.text: How should a sub-agent handle a failed tool call?
options: Return the error as a tool_result so it can reason and try alternatives
audit-a09.explanation: Graceful error handling keeps the pipeline robust.

audit-a09.text: The orchestration model scales auditing by…
options: Composing focused agents into an org-wide, repeatable pipeline
audit-a09.explanation: Composition turns single-control checks into organizational coverage.

audit-a10.text: Why does report quality matter so much?
options: A finding is only as useful as the report that communicates it
audit-a10.explanation: The report is the product that drives remediation.

audit-a10.text: A complete audit finding states which four things?
audit-a10.explanation: What was found, the standard, why, and the resulting risk.

audit-a10.text: How does Claude help with audit reporting?
options: It drafts structured, consistent findings faster than a human writer
audit-a10.explanation: Report writing is a natural fit for the model's strengths.

audit-a10.text: A good report serves which audiences?
options: Executives (summary, risk) and engineers (specific, actionable remediation)
audit-a10.explanation: Layer an exec summary over actionable technical detail.

options: A specific remediation the team can implement, not a vague 'fix it'
audit-a10.explanation: Concrete remediation steps get findings fixed.

audit-a10.text: Why assign severity to each finding?
options: So teams remediate the highest-impact issues first
audit-a10.explanation: Severity drives prioritization of limited remediation effort.

audit-a10.text: Each finding in the report should include…
options: The supporting evidence that substantiates it
audit-a10.explanation: Evidence is what turns a finding from opinion into fact.

options: Just the work — the report is what delivers the value
audit-a10.explanation: The report communicates the risk and drives action.

audit-a11.text: Why move from annual audits to continuous monitoring?
options: Annual audits find last year's problems; continuous monitoring finds today's
audit-a11.explanation: Continuous checking catches issues as they appear, not months later.

audit-a11.text: How does a sentinel agent achieve continuous coverage?
options: It runs on a schedule (e.g. cron) re-checking controls automatically

audit-a11.text: What does continuous monitoring detect that point-in-time audits miss?
options: Control drift — a control falling out of compliance between audits
audit-a11.explanation: Drift detection is the core benefit of continuous monitoring.

audit-a11.text: When a sentinel agent finds a deviation, it should…
options: Alert the right team promptly so it's remediated fast
audit-a11.explanation: Fast alerting shrinks the window of exposure.

audit-a11.text: Continuous monitoring works by comparing current state to…
audit-a11.explanation: Baseline comparison is how deviations are surfaced.

audit-a11.text: Many breaches stayed undetected for months because…
options: No continuous monitoring caught the deviation that enabled them
audit-a11.explanation: Continuous monitoring is the control that shortens dwell time.

options: Far more controls, far more often, than periodic manual review

audit-a11.text: The sentinel model changes audit from…
options: A periodic snapshot to a continuous stream of assurance
audit-a11.explanation: Continuous monitoring is the modern security posture.

audit-a12.text: What does the full agentic audit pipeline promise?
options: Define scope, press run, receive report — end to end
audit-a12.explanation: The capstone composes discovery, analysis, evidence, and reporting.

audit-a12.text: The full pipeline combines which stages?
options: Discovery, analysis, evidence collection, and report generation
audit-a12.explanation: It chains every capability from the epoch into one flow.

audit-a12.text: Where do humans stay in the loop of a full pipeline?
options: Reviewing and approving findings before action, especially high-impact ones
audit-a12.explanation: Humans validate findings and authorize consequential actions.

audit-a12.text: Across the pipeline, permissions should be…
options: Scoped per agent to the minimum each stage requires
audit-a12.explanation: Least privilege applies to every agent in the pipeline.

audit-a12.text: A key benefit of the full pipeline is…
options: The same comprehensive audit re-runs on demand with no extra labor
It must be rebuilt each time
audit-a12.explanation: Define once, run anytime — coverage scales with compute.

audit-a12.text: How do you keep a full pipeline trustworthy?
options: Validate tool inputs/outputs, test the agents, and review findings
audit-a12.explanation: Validation, testing, and review keep the pipeline reliable.

audit-a12.text: The full pipeline lets a small team…
options: Replace all security with one prompt
audit-a12.explanation: Composed agents give small teams org-scale coverage.

audit-a12.text: The epoch's thesis is that agentic audit is…
options: Continuous, scalable, and repeatable — not point-in-time and manual
audit-a12.explanation: Define scope, press run, receive report — continuously.
